title: Staking up and tying off
subtitle: Setting the upright stakes and finishing the rim on a willow basket
excerpt: Staking up means inserting the upright stakes that will carry the basket sides, bending them vertical, and locking them with the first wale. Tying off means finishing the rim with a four-rod border that locks every stake permanently. These two steps bookend every willow basket and the quality of both determines whether the finished basket is tight and durable or loose and short-lived.
sourceNotes: Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for willow basket construction and border technique. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the four-rod border.

content.text: The
stakes
go into the base weave, get bent upright, and are locked by the first
wale
before side weaving starts. If the stakes lean or space unevenly here, the finished basket will be uneven no matter how well the body is woven. The closing
border
matters just as much: a loose or mis-sequenced border opens in use and lets the stakes pull free. Both steps apply to every willow basket regardless of size.
What it does

Staking up converts a flat base into the starting structure for the sides. Tying off converts the loose stake ends at the top of the woven sides into a firm, plaited rim that cannot open under the weight of the basket's contents.
When to use it
At the start and end of every willow basket regardless of form or size. The same staking-up and four-rod border apply to round, oval, and square-based baskets.
What you need
Soaked stakes (4 to 5 mm diameter, matching the base dimensions). A rapping iron to pack the first wale. A sloyd knife to point stake butts before insertion and to trim ends at the border.
Safety
Point stake ends away from the body when cutting. Keep the rapping iron's stroke directed downward.
Staking up
Step 1: Point and insert stakes

Sharpen the butt of each stake to a slanted point with the sloyd knife. Push one stake into the base weave on each side of every base stick, angled downward into the base at about 45 degrees. The point slides between the weaving rods rather than breaking them. Two stakes per base stick is standard; insert them tight against the base stick on alternate sides.
Step 2: Bend stakes upright

Hold each stake between thumb and forefinger at the point where it exits the weave. Press the thumb firmly against the base-side of the stake at this point and bend the stake upright in one smooth motion; the thumb-pressure creates a clean kink at the base rather than a gradual curve that would make the base-to-side angle soft.
Step 3: Weave the first wale

Insert three waling rods behind three consecutive stakes. Weave the leftmost rod in front of two stakes and behind the third; then advance to the rod on its left. A full four-rod wale needs a fourth rod behind the fourth stake. Continue around the full circumference, packing down with the rapping iron after every five stakes. This first wale locks the upright stakes at the correct spacing and angle.
Tying off (four-rod border)
Step 1: Start the border sequence

The border is worked with the basket in front of the operator, the rim at a comfortable working height. Take the first four stakes and bend each one down behind the stake to its right; hold them in position with a clothes peg or finger. These four bent stakes are the starting set for the four-rod sequence.
Step 2: Work the border sequence

For each upright stake in turn: bend it down to the right, in front of the next two upright stakes, behind the third, and tuck the tip down inside the basket through the body weave at this point. The four bent working strands advance one stake at a time around the rim. Each stake goes in front of two uprights and behind one before being tucked in. Continue until all stakes are worked into the border.
Step 3: Close the border

The last four stakes are worked through the starting loops of the first four stakes to close the border. Thread each one under the starting loop of its corresponding early stake, maintaining the over-two, under-one sequence. Trim all protruding ends flush with the sloyd knife on the inside of the basket.
What it looks like when it is right
A clean plaited rope around the rim, with all stakes disappearing into the border from the outside. No stake ends visible on the outside face. The rim is firm and does not flex when pressed.
Common mistakes
Stakes not bent with a firm kink at the base: they lean and produce a flared-out basket rather than vertical sides. The thumb-press kink at staking-up is the fix.
Border sequence lost midway around: count the working stakes after every five stakes worked to check the sequence is still in four-rod order. If it is wrong, unweave back to the last correct point.
